text: The surgical team prepares for a biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch by placing four 8mm trocars and a 12mm stapling trocar in the right mid-clavicular line. The Nathanson liver retractor is positioned, and the cecum is identified. The ileum is measured back 150 centimeters to form the common channel, marked with clips for anatomical orientation. Clips are placed distally and proximally on the ileum, which is then run another 200 centimeters to establish a total alimentary limb of 350 centimeters. A stitch and clip are placed to orient the ileum for the duodenal ileal anastomosis. The bowel is positioned near the 8mm trocar at the right mid-axillary line, facilitating the creation of the anastomosis. The ileum is tacked to the abdominal wall, allowing the robot to be docked for the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon performs a sleeve gastrectomy using a 36 French bougie, starting with a green load 60mm stapler and continuing with blue loads. The orogastric tube is retracted, and the duodenal division begins. Vessels and peritoneal reflection are taken cephalad to the duodenum with minimal dissection to preserve blood supply. A Ray-Tec is used for hemostasis if bleeding occurs. The duodenum is lifted and anteriorly scooped under direct visualization, with a Penrose Drain placed around it. A 60mm SureForm stapler blue load cartridge is used to transect the duodenum, ensuring only the duodenum is across the anvil and cartridge. The staple line is inspected for hemostasis. The bowel is aligned to the duodenum for a double-layer hand-sewn duodenal ileostomy, starting with a 3-0 Stratafix suture for the posterior staple line, followed by an enterotomy and inner layer with 3-0 Vicryl suture, and completing the outer layer with Stratafix. The anastomosis is made wide, matching the width of the duodenum and ileum.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and creates a window in the mesentery of the biliopancreatic limb, using a white load, 60mm stapler to transect it. The roux limb is run backwards to locate the clips, aligning the biliopancreatic limb with the common channel or alimentary limb for the creation of a side-to-side, 60mm enteroenterostomy. Scissors are used to make precise enterotomies, and the mesenteric defect is closed. A methylene blue leak test and air leak test are performed, followed by the removal of the stomach fundus and antrum, with closure of the 12mm port site.
